Q1: I tried to keep a pretty strict routine, honestly. With four online courses and limited resources, I had to be organized about it. I'd block out time in the mornings before my other responsibilities kicked in, usually around 22 hours spread across the week. I'd make lists and check things off systematically because that's the only way I could keep track of everything. It wasn't glamorous, but it kept me moving through the material.
Q2: I really tried to stick to regular times because my life is complicated enough without adding chaos to my study schedule. Being 29 with limited financial resources means I have other things pulling at my time constantly. But honestly, when deadlines approached, everything would shift and I'd have to cram more into certain days. The routine was more of an ideal than a reality some weeks.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new assignments posted or seeing what was due next. I'd log in to make sure I wasn't missing anything important. Sometimes the gamified tasks would send notifications and I'd pop in to complete those because they felt manageable. I wasn't browsing around much—I was there with a purpose, to tick things off my list.
Q4: Yeah, logging in became almost automatic, like checking email in the morning. I'd do it with my coffee before anything else. The gamified elements actually helped make it feel less like a chore and more like something I could accomplish quickly. After a few weeks, it was just part of my morning whether I wanted it to be or not.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were my lifeline because that's how I learn best. I could download PDFs and work through them at my own pace, highlighting and taking notes. The gamified tasks were also helpful because they broke things into smaller pieces that felt achievable. Watching nearly 280 minutes of video helped too, but honestly I preferred reading.
Q6: I completely ignored the discussion forums. Zero posts from me. I just couldn't bring myself to engage there—it felt performative and I didn't have the energy for it. Some of the peer interaction stuff felt forced too. When you're stretched thin on resources and time, you prioritize what directly impacts your grade, and discussions didn't seem essential.
Q7: If I could see how it connected to an assignment or the exam, it felt worthwhile. The gamified tasks were good because they gave immediate feedback and that little dopamine hit of completion. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly tied to my success felt like a waste of my limited time and energy.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford. Required ones got done no matter what—that's why my assignment completion was so high. But optional stuff? I'd look at it and think about all the other things demanding my attention and just couldn't justify the time. It's not that I didn't want to learn more, it's that I had to be strategic.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded everything I could and studied offline constantly. With limited resources, I couldn't always rely on having good internet access, so I'd save PDFs and read them on my phone during commutes or breaks. I had notebooks full of handwritten notes that never showed up in any system. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the screen.
Q10: Most of my deep studying happened offline with printed materials and handwritten notes. I'd read something once online, download it, and then spend hours with it later in ways the LMS couldn't track. There were whole weekends where I was buried in course content but the system would show nothing because I wasn't connected.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture video or reading once, download or take extensive notes, and then never open it again online. The LMS probably thought I barely engaged with certain materials, but I'd have pages of notes proving otherwise. It was more efficient for me to work offline than to keep logging back in.
Q12: There were definitely low points. The stress was really high this semester and sometimes I just felt sad and overwhelmed. When you're dealing with financial constraints and trying to manage four courses, some days you just can't bring yourself to engage meaningfully. My attendance dropped to about 33% because sometimes I just couldn't show up, physically or mentally.
Q13: Massively. The workload across four courses was brutal, and being stressed made everything harder. Some days I was so fatigued I'd log in, stare at the screen, and accomplish nothing. My limited resources meant I sometimes had to choose between studying and other necessities. The LMS got neglected when life got too heavy.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd open videos and let them play while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. I'd scroll through readings without absorbing anything. The system would show I was active, but I was just going through the motions because I was too exhausted to actually engage.
Q15: I avoided the exam prep more than I should have. I kept telling myself I'd get to it, but the assignments felt more urgent and concrete. Looking at my exam score of 41, that was clearly a mistake. I think I was scared of how much I didn't know, so I just kept putting it off and focusing on what I could control—the assignments.
Q16: Deadlines were everything. My entire LMS usage spiked around due dates. I'd log in more frequently, watch videos I'd been putting off, and frantically complete tasks. Without a deadline, I probably wouldn't have engaged much at all. The gamified tasks helped because they created mini-deadlines that kept me coming back.
Q17: When I got feedback on assignments, it motivated me to keep going because my scores were decent—around 83%. That positive reinforcement helped on the dark days. But the exam feedback was devastating and honestly made me want to disengage completely. It's hard to stay motivated when you fail despite trying so hard on everything else.
Q18: The structure helped and hurt. Having clear deadlines and organized modules made it easier to follow along systematically. But the course didn't seem to account for students like me who were juggling multiple courses with limited resources. The attendance requirements were particularly hard to meet given my circumstances.
Q19: The gamified elements made engagement easier—they broke things into achievable chunks. But the heavy reliance on live attendance made things harder for me. Also, the discussion requirements felt designed for students with more time and energy than I had. More flexibility would have helped students in my situation.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was logging in frequently, watching videos, completing tasks—but my exam performance didn't reflect that activity. Maybe they're engaging with the system but not actually learning deeply. Or maybe, like me, they're doing everything except what actually matters for the final grade. It's easy to feel busy without being effective.
Q21: That's the opposite of me, but I can see how it happens. Some people just learn differently or more efficiently. They might study offline, use external resources, or just be naturally better at exams. The LMS doesn't capture intelligence or prior knowledge. Someone could barely log in but ace everything because they already knew the material.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, task completion rates. But they miss the quality of engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was crying while submitting an assignment or if I understood anything I read. They show behavior, not learning. And they completely miss offline studying.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It means your brain is active and you're making connections. For me this semester, I was going through the motions more than truly engaging. I completed almost everything but I don't think I was really engaged—I was just surviving.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was trying despite everything working against me. It wouldn't show the hours of offline studying, the stress I was under, or how sad I felt when I failed the exam despite completing 96% of assignments. It can't capture that I'm a 29-year-old with limited resources doing my absolute best. Numbers don't tell that story.
